title: The Fall of the Berlin Wall

lines.text: In August 1989, the opening of the Iron Curtain between Austria and Hungary set off a chain reaction that would eventually lead to the fall of the Berlin Wall.
Tens of thousands of East Germans took advantage of this newfound freedom, traveling to Hungary and then making their way to West Germany.
lines.image_keyword: East German refugees leaving Prague for Bavaria

lines.text: The Berlin Wall was a formidable barrier, stretching 155 kilometers across the city and topped with minefields and watchtowers.
Despite its imposing presence, the wall had become increasingly porous, with thousands of people making their way to the West every year.
lines.image_keyword: East Germans crossing Berlin Wall illegally

lines.text: On October 18, 1989, longtime leader Erich Honecker stepped down in favor of Egon Krenz, who promised reforms but failed to deliver.
As the political situation continued to deteriorate, thousands took to the streets demanding change and calling for Krenz's resignation.
Finally, on November 9, 1989, the government announced it would allow East Germans to travel to the West without restriction, marking the beginning of the end of the wall.
In the end, the fall of the Berlin Wall marked a major turning point in world history, as the Iron Curtain was lifted and a new era of freedom and cooperation began.
